summary: Gruber reviews Apple's September 2019 event covering Apple Arcade, Apple TV+, iPad, Apple Watch Series 5, and the iPhone 11 lineup. He praises the $5/month pricing for both Arcade and TV+, calling the bundling of free TV+ with hardware purchases a brilliant strategy that reinforces Apple as a hardware company. He highlights the always-on Apple Watch display as a long-awaited feature and analyzes the iPhone 11's camera strategy of choosing ultra-wide over telephoto. He finds the 'Pro' naming appropriate despite minimal hardware differences from the standard iPhone 11, and notes the mysterious U1 chip as a harbinger of future features.
key_points: Apple TV+ bundled free with hardware purchases is a clever strategy that positions it as an Amazon Prime-like perk rather than a Netflix competitor, reinforcing Apple's identity as a hardware company
The iPhone 11's choice of ultra-wide over telephoto as its second lens represents a bet on computational photography — it's easier to zoom digitally than compute a wider field of view
The iPhone 11 Pro's 4-5 hour battery life improvement over XS models seems extraordinary and likely stems from a more efficient OLED display and improved battery chemistry
The always-on Apple Watch display was an inevitable but welcome surprise, and the $199 Series 3 price point could drive significant holiday sales
The U1 ultra-wideband chip is clearly designed for more than just AirDrop pointing — likely related to rumored tile trackers — but Apple isn't ready to reveal its full purpose yet
money_quotes: Apple, for all its talk about 'Services, Services, Services', is and will always remain a hardware company at heart.
It turns TV+ into a perk just for being an Apple customer.
Fundamentally it's a bet on the power of computational photography — it's easier to zoom in digitally than it is to compute a wider field of view.
The goal should be to make it appear that the camera can see in dim lighting, not to make dark scenes look brightly lit.
Some folks will look at that list and say the iPhones 11 Pro aren't really 'pro'. I look at that list and say the regular iPhone 11 is almost just as 'pro' at significantly lower prices.
That sort of improvement year-over-year seems bananas.
In person, it's a pretty neutral green — has a bit of a Boba Fett feel to it.
That's a fine feature, but not worth an entire chip.
key_insight: Apple's decision to bundle TV+ free with hardware purchases reveals that despite its services narrative, Apple's core strategy remains making hardware ownership the most rewarding ecosystem choice.
